title: Eating Disorders: Anorexia and Bulimia
subtitle: The nurse cares for a patient with an eating disorder — telling anorexia from bulimia, supervising meals, monitoring electrolytes and heart rhythm, and watching for refeeding syndrome.

patient.name: Eating Disorders
patient.admitDate: The nurse supports a patient with an eating disorder.

labs.v: Binge then purge; often normal weight.

nursesNote: The nurse cares for a patient with an eating disorder. Anorexia nervosa is marked by food restriction, an intense fear of gaining weight, a very low body weight, and a distorted body image; medical effects include bradycardia, low blood pressure and temperature, amenorrhea, fine downy hair (lanugo), and electrolyte and bone problems. Bulimia nervosa involves recurrent binge eating followed by purging (self-induced vomiting, laxatives, or excessive exercise), often at a near-normal weight, with signs such as dental enamel erosion, calluses on the knuckles (Russell's sign), and swollen parotid glands. Both disorders can cause dangerous electrolyte imbalances, especially low potassium from purging, which raises the risk of cardiac dysrhythmias, so the nurse monitors electrolytes and heart rhythm. Mealtimes are supervised: the nurse stays with the patient during meals and for about an hour afterward to discourage purging or hiding food, and weighs the patient consistently (same time, in a gown, after voiding) while watching for hidden weights or water-loading. A calm, firm, consistent approach with clear limits avoids power struggles over food. Care also addresses the underlying feelings, control issues, and body-image distortion through therapy and support, not just intake. When a severely malnourished patient is refed, the nurse watches for refeeding syndrome (dangerous drops in phosphate, potassium, and magnesium), advancing nutrition slowly with close monitoring.
providerOrders: Distinguish anorexia from bulimia and assess medical stability
Monitor electrolytes (especially potassium) and heart rhythm
Supervise meals and stay with the patient after eating
Weigh the patient consistently and watch for hidden weights
Advance nutrition slowly and monitor for refeeding syndrome

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about eating disorders?

opts.text: Anorexia involves food restriction and low body weight
Bulimia involves bingeing then purging
Purging can cause low potassium
Eating disorders are only about food
Anorexia can cause bradycardia and low temperature
Supervising meals is part of care
Stay with the patient about an hour after meals
Low potassium raises dysrhythmia risk
Power struggles over food are helpful
Weigh at the same time, in a gown, after voiding
Refeeding too fast can drop phosphate and potassium
Care should address body image and feelings

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order admitting a malnourished patient with anorexia

opts.text: Assess vital signs, electrolytes, and heart rhythm
Establish a structured eating plan with supervision
Advance nutrition slowly to prevent refeeding syndrome
Monitor weight consistently and watch for purging
Address body image and arrange therapy
Very low body weight and lanugo
Dental erosion and knuckle calluses
Refusing meals and intense fear of weight gain
Binge eating followed by self-induced vomiting
Bradycardia, low blood pressure, amenorrhea
Near-normal weight with swollen parotid glands

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient with purging has K 2.7 and an irregular heartbeat
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Place the patient on a cardiac monitor
Recognize hypokalemia as the likely cause
Notify the provider of the low potassium
Give potassium replacement as ordered
Recheck potassium and document
Staying with the patient after meals
Letting the patient skip weights when upset
Monitoring potassium and heart rhythm
Refeeding rapidly to restore weight fast
Using firm, consistent limits without arguing
Focusing only on calories, not feelings
Weighing at the same time in a gown after voiding
Ignoring calluses on the knuckles

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Psychosocial Integrity, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
